items.fix: Negate the priority on push and pop, or use `heapq.nlargest`.
items.why: `heapq` only implements a min-heap; without the flip you serve the *lowest* priority first.
items.example:
```python
import heapq
h = []
heapq.heappush(h, (-score, item))   # behaves as max-heap by score
neg, item = heapq.heappop(h)
best = -neg
```

items.mistake: Sorting the whole list to extract top-k.
items.fix: Use `heapq.nlargest(k, items)` or a size-k heap.
items.why: Full sort is O(n log n); a size-k heap solves it in O(n log k) — far cheaper when k ≪ n.
items.example:
```python
import heapq
top = heapq.nlargest(10, chunks, key=lambda c: c.score)
```

items.mistake: Pushing tuples whose secondary element is unorderable.
items.fix: Add a tie-breaker (timestamp, counter) before the un-orderable payload, or wrap with `@dataclass(order=True)`.
items.why: When two priorities tie, Python compares the next tuple element; comparing two unhashable/unorderable objects raises `TypeError`.
items.example:
```python
import itertools, heapq
c = itertools.count()
heapq.heappush(pq, (priority, next(c), payload_obj))
```

items.mistake: Trying to remove an arbitrary element with `list.remove` + `heapify`.
items.fix: Use 'lazy deletion': mark entries as removed in a side set and skip them on pop.
items.why: Searching + re-heapifying is O(n); lazy deletion keeps push/pop O(log n) on average.
